results.title: UN envoy seeks to salvage Yemen’s largest prisoner swap after setback
results.description: UN envoy seeks to salvage Yemen’s largest prisoner swap after setback The postponement has cast fresh doubt over one of the few humanitarian initiatives that has continued to bridge Yemen’s deeply divided rivals. Sunday 12/07/2026 United Nations envoy for Yemen Hans Grundberg speaks during a news conference in Sana’a, April 13, 2022. SANA’A The United Nations moved on Saturday to prevent the collapse of Yemen’s biggest planned prisoner exchange after the government and the Houthi movement renewed their commitment to the agreement despite postponing its implementation at the last minute. UN Special Envoy Hans Grundberg said both sides had reaffirmed during the past 48 hours that they intended to honour the May agreement covering the release of more than 1,600 detainees and had pledged to complete the remaining technical and operational preparations. His intervention came after Friday’s announcement that the exchange, due to take place on Saturday, had been delayed indefinitely, with the government and the Houthis trading accusations over responsibility for the setback. The postponement has cast fresh doubt over one of the few humanitarian initiatives that has continued to bridge Yemen’s deeply divided rivals, even as broader political negotiations remain stalled. The International Committee of the Red Cross, which has coordinated previous exchanges, said it stood ready to facilitate the transfer and safe return of detainees once the parties completed the necessary arrangements. The organisation urged both sides to press ahead with efforts to implement the agreement, describing the operation as a humanitarian priority that would allow hundreds of families to be reunited after years of separation. Diplomats regard prisoner exchanges as one of the few confidence-building measures capable of sustaining dialogue between the rivals while negotiations over a broader political settlement remain deadlocked. The United Nations has repeatedly argued that successful implementation of humanitarian agreements can help create momentum for progress on other contentious issues, including ceasefire arrangements and political talks. The agreement reached in Amman in May followed three months of negotiations and envisages the release of around 1,700 detainees, including seven Saudi nationals and 20 Sudanese personnel from the Saudi-led coalition. Although both parties insist they remain committed to carrying out the exchange, the delay illustrates the operational difficulties and enduring mistrust that continue to hamper mediation efforts after more than a decade of conflict. The prisoner issue remains one of the most emotionally charged aspects of the war, with thousands of Yemeni families still awaiting news of relatives held by opposing sides. International mediators hope the eventual implementation of the exchange will restore confidence in humanitarian negotiations and help preserve the limited diplomatic channels that remain open between the two adversaries. Suggested By Editor Yemen edges towards fresh fighting as fragile truce unravels Suggested By Editor Yemen edges towards fresh fighting as fragile truce unravels

results.title: West Afghanistan female dress-code crackdown hits businesses
results.description: HERAT: Businesses in Afghanistan’s western city of Herat have suffered from a downturn in female customers opting to stay home following a recent crackdown by morality police on women’s attire, according to shopkeepers, drivers and residents. Dozens of women were detained by the Taliban government’s morality police in early June on accusations they were violating official dress codes by not wearing the body-cloaking chador or burqa. A rare protest against the restrictions was violently dispersed with at least two people killed, according to the United Nations.

results.title: Iran’s absent supreme leader pledges revenge for slain father
results.description: Iran’s absent supreme leader pledges revenge for slain father “Whether we are there or not, this will be accomplished, and soon every free person around the world will fulfill a part of this divine mission,” Mojtaba’s statement said. Sunday 12/07/2026 A woman holds a poster depicting Iran’s new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei during a rally in Tehran, March 26, 2026. TEHRAN A written statement from Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei threatened vengeance for the death of his predecessor and father on Saturday, but added that it would depend not only on Iran but also on “free people around the world.” In the supreme leader’s first public message since funeral ceremonies for his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, began a week ago, the statement read on state television said that vengeance was “the demand of the nation” and “must certainly” take place. Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed in a US-Israeli airstrike on February 28, at the start of the war. “We pledge to avenge the blood of the martyred leader and all the martyrs of these two wars from the criminal and disgraced killers,” the statement said. Mojtaba Khamenei, who senior sources have said suffered facial disfigurement and other injuries in the strike, has not been seen by Iranians since he was appointed supreme leader on March 8. “Whether we are there or not, this will be accomplished, and soon every free person around the world will fulfill a part of this divine mission,” the statement said. An exchange of attacks between US and Iranian forces this week has raised doubts over a truce agreed between Washington and Tehran aimed at ending the four-month war. Iran says the deal will ultimately deliver major economic benefits. Despite the recent flare-up, US President Donald Trump, while declaring that the ceasefire was over, said on Friday that the two countries had agreed to continue talks. Mojtaba Khamenei’s continued absence from the public eye; there has been no photo, video or audio recording of him published since the air attack; has added to the uncertainties facing Iran, with some Iranians saying the new leader must be seen even if he is injured. He became supreme leader with the backing of the powerful Revolutionary Guards. Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who ruled for 37 years, was buried in the country’s holiest shrine, state media said on Friday, after huge crowds gathered for his funeral.
